item.title: AMD's NPUs will finally get support from Windows' Task Manager
item.itunes:title: AMD's NPUs will finally get support from Windows' Task Manager
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Microsoft will soon support AMD NPUs in Task Manager.
  • NPUs aid AI-based processes, separate from CPUs and GPUs.
  • NPU implementation is important for monitoring and optimizing system performance when training or using AI.

For a little while now, Microsoft and Intel have enjoyed a good partnership. DirectML is coming to Intel NPUs, and for a little while, those with AMD processors were seemingly left in the cold. Now, Microsoft has finally shown the Intel rival some attention by giving AMD support for the Task Manager.

Related
AMD Ryzen 7 8700G review: The best gaming CPU for those on a budget
Farewell, discrete graphics card

AMD NPU support is making its way to Microsoft's Task Manager

As reported by Neowin, AMD NPUs will finally show up in Windows' Task Manager. If you're not sure what an NPU is, it's a new component to computers to aid with processing. Much like how the CPU handles regular processes and GPUs are dedicated graphics processors, NPUs are specially used for AI-based processes.

With this agreement, AMD's NPUs will now use the Microsoft Compute Driver Model (MCDM). Part of this implementation means you can keep tabs on how the NPU is doing and how much processing power it's using via Task Manager, which will be particularly important when people begin using their NPUs for strenuous AI-based tasks. As AMD puts it:

Being able to track how resources are allocated in real-time and which system components are under load is useful for monitoring application behavior. This kind of tracking is particularly important in notebooks where end users may choose to maximize battery life by controlling where certain workloads run or adjusting global power settings either via the Windows Settings menu or in an OEM-provided application.

NPU implementation is going to be vital in the coming months. With AI PCs entering the consumer market, Microsoft will ideally support as many different NPU brands as possible so people aren't left out because of their choice of hardware.

item.title: 10 mistakes you should not make when building a new PC for the first time
item.itunes:title: 10 mistakes you should not make when building a new PC for the first time
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Choose the right PSU: Calculate your needs, prioritize efficiency and safety, and invest in a reputable, efficiently rated PSU for a reliable and durable build.
  • Check for compatibility: Verify case and cooler clearance, RAM and cooler interference, motherboard connectors, CPU socket compatibility, and expansion slot and storage interface support.
  • Pay attention to details: Remove protective covers, properly install the I/O shield and standoffs, and avoid mixing up power connectors to prevent damage and ensure optimal performance.

Building your PC over opting for prebuilts off the shelf is often recommended due to the better value for money, more tailored specifications, and, frankly, the sheer joy of the process. However, diving into your first build can be a minefield of potential mistakes. From choosing an incompatible mix of components to overlooking the need for thermal paste, the devil is in the details. This guide aims to guide you through common mistakes, ensuring your journey from parts selection to the final boot is as smooth as possible.

Related
5 PC building myths that somehow still exist
PC building has come a long way, but some myths just refuse to die

10 Choosing the wrong PSU

The be quiet! Dark Power Pro 13 power supply.

A well-chosen PSU ensures stability and longevity, safeguarding your investment in high-performance components. Here's a concise guide to navigating this critical decision without falling into the trap of overspending or underspending:

  • Calculate your needs: Start by listing all your components to determine the total wattage required, adding a 20%-30% buffer for future upgrades and peak performance. You can also use online calculators to do this.
  • Efficiency matters: Opt for a PSU with a higher efficiency rating. More often than not a PSU with a Gold rating is enough for most builds for energy savings, reduced heat output, and improved system stability.
  • Safety first: Ensure your PSU features essential protections like Over-Voltage, Under-Voltage, and Short-Circuit Protection. Renowned brands often provide these safeguards, contributing to a reliable and durable build.
  • Don't overlook quality: While cutting corners on your PSU to allocate budget elsewhere is tempting, remember that a subpar unit can compromise your entire system. Investing in a reputable, efficiently rated PSU is crucial for the longevity and reliability of your PC.

9 Not checking for compatibility

While planning your first PC build you should spend a substantial amount of time researching the components that you shortlist for your build. Not checking for compatibility can easily become a financial disaster once the components are delivered, and you discover the incompatibility while building the PC. Here are essential compatibility checks to guide your build:
  • Case and cooler clearance: Verify that your case can accommodate the height of your CPU cooler and the length of your GPU. High-performance air coolers and expansive GPUs demand significant space.
  • RAM and cooler interference: Bulky CPU coolers may overlap RAM slots. Ensure your memory modules fit beneath or opt for low-profile RAM if clearance is tight.
  • Motherboard connectors: Match your case's front panel connectors with the motherboard's available headers. Essential for functionality like USB-C ports or even Thunderbolt in some cases.
  • CPU socket compatibility: The motherboard and CPU must share a compatible socket type (e.g., AM4 & AM5 for AMD, LGA1700 for Intel). First-time builders can mess up a bit in this regard, especially on the Intel platform.
  • Expansion slot and storage interface: Check motherboard support for your desired number and type of GPUs or other expansion cards, and ensure it has sufficient SATA or NVMe slots for your storage needs.
  • BIOS compatibility: Some newer CPUs may require a BIOS update to work with an older motherboard. Verify compatibility or plan for a potential update. Most motherboards today come with a BIOS flash utility, which means all you need to update your BIOS will be a USB drive with the updated BIOS and a PSU to power the 24-pin and EPS connector on the motherboard. Simply plug in the drive in the designated USB port and press the button for the flashing.

Addressing these compatibility concerns early prevents potential roadblocks, ensuring a smooth building process and a working PC. You can use platforms like PC Partpicker while planning your build to avoid any potential compatibility issues.

8 Not checking the components before putting them into the case

Before integrating components into your case, pre-assembly testing, commonly known as "breadboarding," is crucial. This process identifies DOA (Dead on Arrival) components, ensuring compatibility and functionality. Here's how to conduct a thorough preliminary check:
  • Start with essential components: Assemble the motherboard, CPU (with cooler), RAM, and the PSU on a non-conductive surface. For this, your motherboard’s retail box should be sufficient. This minimal setup is your starting point for breadboarding, allowing you to test basic functionality without the complexity of a full build.
  • Basic building: Properly seat the CPU without applying force, apply thermal paste, and attach the cooler for optimal heat management. Place the RAM in the ideal slot and press until it clicks, indicating a secure fit. For PCs requiring a discrete GPU, install it in the primary PCIe slot, ensuring it's locked in place, and connect any necessary power cables from the PSU. Next, you can connect your storage devices, for M.2 SSDs. It is ideal to place them before installing the GPU. That way you don't have to remove it if the M.2 slot falls under the graphics card.
  • Power on and test: With the basic build done, you can power on the setup. Listen for beep codes (if an onboard buzzer is equipped) or look for debug LEDs (if available) that indicate the status of each component. These signals can help diagnose non-functional parts or compatibility issues​​​​.
  • Check BIOS/UEFI: If the system posts, enter the BIOS/UEFI to verify that all installed hardware is recognized correctly. This step is essential to confirm the functionality of your components and their readiness for the final build. Pay special attention to the reported RAM and storage capacities.
  • Use Anti-static precautions: Throughout the process, use anti-static gloves or a wrist strap to prevent static damage to sensitive components​.

Breadboarding is an important step in the PC building process, offering a chance to address issues before they become more significant problems in a fully assembled system. This methodical approach saves time and effort, ensuring a smooth and successful build.

7 Forgetting to remove protective covers

While assembling a PC, removing protective covers on components is a detail that, though small, holds a significant impact on both performance and aesthetics. Components like CPU coolers, GPUs, RAM, and even cases often come with plastic films or covers designed to protect against scratches and dust during transit. However, overlooking the removal of these protective layers can lead to adverse effects post-assembly.

For instance, a common oversight is leaving the plastic film on the underside of CPU coolers. This mistake can severely impede thermal transfer, leading to alarmingly high temperatures and potentially shortening the lifespan of your CPU. Similarly, GPUs and RAM sticks may also feature protective stickers or films that, if not removed, could restrict airflow or heat dissipation, affecting performance and cooling efficiency.

Beyond performance implications, the aesthetic aspect of your build can also suffer. Protective films on cases or transparent side panels, if not peeled off, detract from the visual appeal of your setup, leaving it looking unfinished or clouded.

Therefore, as you assemble your PC, meticulously inspect each component for any protective materials. Make it a habit to remove these before installation to ensure your system not only operates at its best but also looks its best. This simple step is crucial for achieving optimal thermal performance and maintaining the aesthetics of your build.

6 Ignoring fan placement, orientation, and dust management

Correct fan positioning and orientation directly impact system temperatures and overall performance. Incorrect fan installation, driven by the desire for aesthetics over functionality, can lead to a setup that functions as a dust magnet due to negative pressure. This common mistake turns your PC into an effective air filter for the room, capturing dust and fur, especially when placed near the floor.

To avoid such scenarios, consider the following guidelines:

  • Fan orientation: Ensure that fans are correctly oriented to support a balanced airflow within the case. Intake fans should draw cool air in, while exhaust fans expel hot air out. Misalignment can cause the recirculation of hot air and increased temperatures.
  • Avoid negative pressure: Striving for a look that showcases the fans' attractive side can inadvertently create negative pressure, drawing in unfiltered air and dust. Aim for a positive or neutral air pressure setup by balancing intake and exhaust fan numbers and placements.
  • Elevate your PC: Keeping your PC off the floor, particularly away from carpeted areas, significantly reduces dust and fur accumulation. An elevated position promotes better airflow and minimizes debris intake.
  • Regular cleaning: Schedule routine cleanings and downtimes to remove accumulated dust from filters, fans, and other components. This maintenance is essential for sustaining airflow efficiency and cooling performance, ensuring your system remains clean, cool, and visually appealing. You can do this using an airblower and a brush but make sure that while applying pressurized air none of the fans spin, failing to do so might cause the fan header to burn out.

By prioritizing effective fan placement and dust management, you can maintain optimal temperatures and longevity for your PC components, all while keeping the system's appearance sharp and dust-free.

5 Forgetting to install the I/O shield

A crucial, yet sometimes overlooked step in PC assembly is the installation of the motherboard I/O shield. This metal plate, often included with your motherboard, plays a vital role in protecting the motherboard against electromagnetic interference (EMI) and static discharge. It also prevents dust and debris from entering the system, which could lead to damage or decreased performance over time. Additionally, an I/O shield gives the back of your computer a neat, finished appearance by covering the unsightly gaps around the I/O ports​​​​​​.

While many modern motherboards come with a pre-installed I/O shield, not all do. If you're working with a motherboard that requires manual I/O shield installation, it's imperative to insert the shield into your case before mounting the motherboard. Failing to do so means you might have to disassemble major parts of your build just to fit the shield in place, wasting time and effort​.

Installation is straightforward, simply align the shield with the case's cut-out from the inside, ensuring the correct orientation by matching the port openings on the shield with those on the motherboard. Apply even pressure until the shield clicks into place. It's essential to ensure that the metal tabs or prongs on the shield lightly touch the metal on the motherboard's I/O panel for proper grounding and to reduce electrical interference​​​​​.

4 Forgetting to properly install standoffs and overtightening screws

Standoffs, typically made of brass, ensure there is no direct contact between the motherboard's circuitry and the metal case, preventing potential short circuits and providing a grounding point. Most cases come with pre-installed standoffs, but if your case doesn't align with your motherboard's holes, you may need to adjust or add standoffs manually​​.

When it comes to screwing the motherboard and components into place, the consensus is to tighten the screws until they are snug but not overly tight. Over-tightening can strip the brass standoffs or damage the motherboard by crushing the PCB. The screws should be turned just enough to hold the board firmly in place without requiring excessive force. This approach prevents potential damage to the motherboard and ensures a secure installation without risking the components.

3 Mixing up 8pin EPS and PCIe power connectors

Mixing up the 8-pin EPS and PCIe power connectors is a common mistake due to their similar appearances. However, they serve different purposes and are not interchangeable. The EPS connector is specifically designed for CPU power on the motherboard, providing the necessary power for the processor to function properly. On the other hand, the PCIe power connector is intended for graphics cards and some PCIe devices, delivering additional power these components need to operate​​​​​.

Despite looking nearly identical, these connectors have key differences in their pin configurations to prevent incorrect connections. The EPS-12V connector typically has two square-shaped pins in one row, while the PCIe 8-pin connector has a different arrangement with one square-shaped pin for pins 5-8, designed to prevent them from fitting into the wrong slot. Attempting to force a connector into the incorrect slot can damage the components​​​.

Forgetting to connect the EPS power can also lead to significant issues, including the computer not booting at all. This connector is often located in a less visible area of the motherboard, possibly obscured by fans or radiators, making it easy to overlook. Realizing this mistake after the system is assembled can result in wasted time and effort, as you may need to disassemble parts of your build to access and connect the EPS power properly​.

Always double-check the labels on your cables and connectors to ensure the correct usage, and remember, when in doubt, refer to your power supply and motherboard manuals for guidance. This careful attention to detail can save you from potential hardware damage and the frustration of troubleshooting preventable power issues.

2 Installing RAM in the wrong slots

Installing RAM in the correct slots on your motherboard is crucial for optimizing the memory bandwidth and taking full advantage of dual or quad-channel memory configurations. The placement of RAM modules directly influences whether your system operates in single, dual, or even quad-channel mode, which in turn affects the overall performance of your PC.

For motherboards with four RAM slots, a common configuration for dual-channel memory is to insert RAM modules into slots 2 and 4. This setup allows the CPU to access the RAM modules faster, thereby enhancing system performance. If you're using a single RAM stick, it's typically advised to install it in the slot that's farthest from the CPU to avoid potential interference with CPU coolers or other components​​​.

For dual-channel RAM, it's important to place matching RAM modules in the correct slots, often indicated by color coding or labeling (e.g., DIMM1, DIMM2, etc.) on the motherboard.

This ensures that the memory modules operate in dual-channel mode, doubling the data transfer rate compared to a single-channel setup. The exact slots can vary depending on the motherboard manufacturer and model, so consulting the motherboard's manual is essential for the correct installation​.

If your motherboard supports more than four RAM slots, indicating potential triple-channel or quad-channel memory support, the slot configuration will differ. In such cases, referring to the motherboard's manual is again crucial to determining the correct installation process for maximizing memory performance.

1 Not budgeting properly for software and peripherals

When planning a PC build, it's crucial not to allocate your entire budget to hardware components alone, neglecting the cost of the operating system (OS), software, and peripherals necessary for a complete setup. A common oversight is underestimating or forgetting to budget for these essential elements, leading to a scenario where you might have a powerful build but lack the software or peripherals to fully utilize it.

Moreover, peripherals such as monitors, keyboards, mice, and possibly speakers or headsets can vary widely in price. Monitors, especially, can be a significant portion of your budget, with prices ranging from $100 to several thousand dollars, depending on the specifications. Keyboards, mice, and headsets also have a broad price range, from around $20-$30 to $200-$300, based on performance and features​.

Therefore, when planning your PC build, it's wise to list all components, software, and peripherals you'll need and allocate your budget accordingly. This holistic approach ensures you don't end up with a high-end PC that you can't fully enjoy due to missing software or peripherals.

Bonus: Not choosing the correct tools and workspace

Choosing the correct tools and workspace is extremely important for a smooth PC building process, ensuring safety and efficiency. To start, a clean, spacious, and well-lit area serves as an ideal workspace, minimizing the risk of losing parts or damaging components. An anti-static mat and wrist strap are essential to prevent electrostatic discharge, protecting sensitive hardware.

For tools, a set of precision screwdrivers, preferably magnetized to handle small screws easily, is necessary. Tweezers or needle-nose pliers can help place or retrieve small items, and scissors or a box cutter are useful for opening packages. Keep cable ties or velcro straps handy for cable management, promoting a tidy build.

Remember, a well-organized workspace and the right set of tools not only facilitate a smoother building experience but also contribute to the overall success and longevity of your custom PC. Ensure you have everything prepared before starting your build to avoid unnecessary interruptions or hardware issues.

Building a PC requires attention to detail, from selecting a power supply that ensures stability and efficiency to ensuring compatibility among all components. Pre-assembly testing, or "breadboarding," is vital for identifying any potential issues before final assembly. Additionally, not removing protective covers or mismanaging fan placement can adversely affect both aesthetics and performance. Equally important is budgeting for not just the hardware but also essential software and peripherals to fully realize the potential of your build. This comprehensive approach ensures a smooth building process, resulting in a system that meets your performance needs and aesthetic preferences, providing a rewarding experience that maximizes both the longevity and efficacy of your custom PC.

4 Forgetting to properly install standoffs and overtightening screws

Standoffs, typically made of brass, ensure there is no direct contact between the motherboard's circuitry and the metal case, preventing potential short circuits and providing a grounding point. Most cases come with pre-installed standoffs, but if your case doesn't align with your motherboard's holes, you may need to adjust or add standoffs manually​​.

When it comes to screwing the motherboard and components into place, the consensus is to tighten the screws until they are snug but not overly tight. Over-tightening can strip the brass standoffs or damage the motherboard by crushing the PCB. The screws should be turned just enough to hold the board firmly in place without requiring excessive force. This approach prevents potential damage to the motherboard and ensures a secure installation without risking the components.

3 Mixing up 8pin EPS and PCIe power connectors

Mixing up the 8-pin EPS and PCIe power connectors is a common mistake due to their similar appearances. However, they serve different purposes and are not interchangeable. The EPS connector is specifically designed for CPU power on the motherboard, providing the necessary power for the processor to function properly. On the other hand, the PCIe power connector is intended for graphics cards and some PCIe devices, delivering additional power these components need to operate​​​​​.

Despite looking nearly identical, these connectors have key differences in their pin configurations to prevent incorrect connections. The EPS-12V connector typically has two square-shaped pins in one row, while the PCIe 8-pin connector has a different arrangement with one square-shaped pin for pins 5-8, designed to prevent them from fitting into the wrong slot. Attempting to force a connector into the incorrect slot can damage the components​​​.

Forgetting to connect the EPS power can also lead to significant issues, including the computer not booting at all. This connector is often located in a less visible area of the motherboard, possibly obscured by fans or radiators, making it easy to overlook. Realizing this mistake after the system is assembled can result in wasted time and effort, as you may need to disassemble parts of your build to access and connect the EPS power properly​.

Always double-check the labels on your cables and connectors to ensure the correct usage, and remember, when in doubt, refer to your power supply and motherboard manuals for guidance. This careful attention to detail can save you from potential hardware damage and the frustration of troubleshooting preventable power issues.

2 Installing RAM in the wrong slots

Installing RAM in the correct slots on your motherboard is crucial for optimizing the memory bandwidth and taking full advantage of dual or quad-channel memory configurations. The placement of RAM modules directly influences whether your system operates in single, dual, or even quad-channel mode, which in turn affects the overall performance of your PC.

For motherboards with four RAM slots, a common configuration for dual-channel memory is to insert RAM modules into slots 2 and 4. This setup allows the CPU to access the RAM modules faster, thereby enhancing system performance. If you're using a single RAM stick, it's typically advised to install it in the slot that's farthest from the CPU to avoid potential interference with CPU coolers or other components​​​.

For dual-channel RAM, it's important to place matching RAM modules in the correct slots, often indicated by color coding or labeling (e.g., DIMM1, DIMM2, etc.) on the motherboard.

This ensures that the memory modules operate in dual-channel mode, doubling the data transfer rate compared to a single-channel setup. The exact slots can vary depending on the motherboard manufacturer and model, so consulting the motherboard's manual is essential for the correct installation​.

If your motherboard supports more than four RAM slots, indicating potential triple-channel or quad-channel memory support, the slot configuration will differ. In such cases, referring to the motherboard's manual is again crucial to determining the correct installation process for maximizing memory performance.

1 Not budgeting properly for software and peripherals

When planning a PC build, it's crucial not to allocate your entire budget to hardware components alone, neglecting the cost of the operating system (OS), software, and peripherals necessary for a complete setup. A common oversight is underestimating or forgetting to budget for these essential elements, leading to a scenario where you might have a powerful build but lack the software or peripherals to fully utilize it.

Moreover, peripherals such as monitors, keyboards, mice, and possibly speakers or headsets can vary widely in price. Monitors, especially, can be a significant portion of your budget, with prices ranging from $100 to several thousand dollars, depending on the specifications. Keyboards, mice, and headsets also have a broad price range, from around $20-$30 to $200-$300, based on performance and features​.

Therefore, when planning your PC build, it's wise to list all components, software, and peripherals you'll need and allocate your budget accordingly. This holistic approach ensures you don't end up with a high-end PC that you can't fully enjoy due to missing software or peripherals.

Bonus: Not choosing the correct tools and workspace

Choosing the correct tools and workspace is extremely important for a smooth PC building process, ensuring safety and efficiency. To start, a clean, spacious, and well-lit area serves as an ideal workspace, minimizing the risk of losing parts or damaging components. An anti-static mat and wrist strap are essential to prevent electrostatic discharge, protecting sensitive hardware.

For tools, a set of precision screwdrivers, preferably magnetized to handle small screws easily, is necessary. Tweezers or needle-nose pliers can help place or retrieve small items, and scissors or a box cutter are useful for opening packages. Keep cable ties or velcro straps handy for cable management, promoting a tidy build.

Remember, a well-organized workspace and the right set of tools not only facilitate a smoother building experience but also contribute to the overall success and longevity of your custom PC. Ensure you have everything prepared before starting your build to avoid unnecessary interruptions or hardware issues.

Building a PC requires attention to detail, from selecting a power supply that ensures stability and efficiency to ensuring compatibility among all components. Pre-assembly testing, or "breadboarding," is vital for identifying any potential issues before final assembly. Additionally, not removing protective covers or mismanaging fan placement can adversely affect both aesthetics and performance. Equally important is budgeting for not just the hardware but also essential software and peripherals to fully realize the potential of your build. This comprehensive approach ensures a smooth building process, resulting in a system that meets your performance needs and aesthetic preferences, providing a rewarding experience that maximizes both the longevity and efficacy of your custom PC.
item.content:encoded:
Key Takeaways

  • Choose the right PSU: Calculate your needs, prioritize efficiency and safety, and invest in a reputable, efficiently rated PSU for a reliable and durable build.
  • Check for compatibility: Verify case and cooler clearance, RAM and cooler interference, motherboard connectors, CPU socket compatibility, and expansion slot and storage interface support.
  • Pay attention to details: Remove protective covers, properly install the I/O shield and standoffs, and avoid mixing up power connectors to prevent damage and ensure optimal performance.

Building your PC over opting for prebuilts off the shelf is often recommended due to the better value for money, more tailored specifications, and, frankly, the sheer joy of the process. However, diving into your first build can be a minefield of potential mistakes. From choosing an incompatible mix of components to overlooking the need for thermal paste, the devil is in the details. This guide aims to guide you through common mistakes, ensuring your journey from parts selection to the final boot is as smooth as possible.

Related
5 PC building myths that somehow still exist
PC building has come a long way, but some myths just refuse to die

10 Choosing the wrong PSU

The be quiet! Dark Power Pro 13 power supply.

A well-chosen PSU ensures stability and longevity, safeguarding your investment in high-performance components. Here's a concise guide to navigating this critical decision without falling into the trap of overspending or underspending:

  • Calculate your needs: Start by listing all your components to determine the total wattage required, adding a 20%-30% buffer for future upgrades and peak performance. You can also use online calculators to do this.
  • Efficiency matters: Opt for a PSU with a higher efficiency rating. More often than not a PSU with a Gold rating is enough for most builds for energy savings, reduced heat output, and improved system stability.
  • Safety first: Ensure your PSU features essential protections like Over-Voltage, Under-Voltage, and Short-Circuit Protection. Renowned brands often provide these safeguards, contributing to a reliable and durable build.
  • Don't overlook quality: While cutting corners on your PSU to allocate budget elsewhere is tempting, remember that a subpar unit can compromise your entire system. Investing in a reputable, efficiently rated PSU is crucial for the longevity and reliability of your PC.

9 Not checking for compatibility

While planning your first PC build you should spend a substantial amount of time researching the components that you shortlist for your build. Not checking for compatibility can easily become a financial disaster once the components are delivered, and you discover the incompatibility while building the PC. Here are essential compatibility checks to guide your build:
  • Case and cooler clearance: Verify that your case can accommodate the height of your CPU cooler and the length of your GPU. High-performance air coolers and expansive GPUs demand significant space.
  • RAM and cooler interference: Bulky CPU coolers may overlap RAM slots. Ensure your memory modules fit beneath or opt for low-profile RAM if clearance is tight.
  • Motherboard connectors: Match your case's front panel connectors with the motherboard's available headers. Essential for functionality like USB-C ports or even Thunderbolt in some cases.
  • CPU socket compatibility: The motherboard and CPU must share a compatible socket type (e.g., AM4 & AM5 for AMD, LGA1700 for Intel). First-time builders can mess up a bit in this regard, especially on the Intel platform.
  • Expansion slot and storage interface: Check motherboard support for your desired number and type of GPUs or other expansion cards, and ensure it has sufficient SATA or NVMe slots for your storage needs.
  • BIOS compatibility: Some newer CPUs may require a BIOS update to work with an older motherboard. Verify compatibility or plan for a potential update. Most motherboards today come with a BIOS flash utility, which means all you need to update your BIOS will be a USB drive with the updated BIOS and a PSU to power the 24-pin and EPS connector on the motherboard. Simply plug in the drive in the designated USB port and press the button for the flashing.

Addressing these compatibility concerns early prevents potential roadblocks, ensuring a smooth building process and a working PC. You can use platforms like PC Partpicker while planning your build to avoid any potential compatibility issues.

8 Not checking the components before putting them into the case

Before integrating components into your case, pre-assembly testing, commonly known as "breadboarding," is crucial. This process identifies DOA (Dead on Arrival) components, ensuring compatibility and functionality. Here's how to conduct a thorough preliminary check:
  • Start with essential components: Assemble the motherboard, CPU (with cooler), RAM, and the PSU on a non-conductive surface. For this, your motherboard’s retail box should be sufficient. This minimal setup is your starting point for breadboarding, allowing you to test basic functionality without the complexity of a full build.
  • Basic building: Properly seat the CPU without applying force, apply thermal paste, and attach the cooler for optimal heat management. Place the RAM in the ideal slot and press until it clicks, indicating a secure fit. For PCs requiring a discrete GPU, install it in the primary PCIe slot, ensuring it's locked in place, and connect any necessary power cables from the PSU. Next, you can connect your storage devices, for M.2 SSDs. It is ideal to place them before installing the GPU. That way you don't have to remove it if the M.2 slot falls under the graphics card.
  • Power on and test: With the basic build done, you can power on the setup. Listen for beep codes (if an onboard buzzer is equipped) or look for debug LEDs (if available) that indicate the status of each component. These signals can help diagnose non-functional parts or compatibility issues​​​​.
  • Check BIOS/UEFI: If the system posts, enter the BIOS/UEFI to verify that all installed hardware is recognized correctly. This step is essential to confirm the functionality of your components and their readiness for the final build. Pay special attention to the reported RAM and storage capacities.
  • Use Anti-static precautions: Throughout the process, use anti-static gloves or a wrist strap to prevent static damage to sensitive components​.

Breadboarding is an important step in the PC building process, offering a chance to address issues before they become more significant problems in a fully assembled system. This methodical approach saves time and effort, ensuring a smooth and successful build.

7 Forgetting to remove protective covers

While assembling a PC, removing protective covers on components is a detail that, though small, holds a significant impact on both performance and aesthetics. Components like CPU coolers, GPUs, RAM, and even cases often come with plastic films or covers designed to protect against scratches and dust during transit. However, overlooking the removal of these protective layers can lead to adverse effects post-assembly.

For instance, a common oversight is leaving the plastic film on the underside of CPU coolers. This mistake can severely impede thermal transfer, leading to alarmingly high temperatures and potentially shortening the lifespan of your CPU. Similarly, GPUs and RAM sticks may also feature protective stickers or films that, if not removed, could restrict airflow or heat dissipation, affecting performance and cooling efficiency.

Beyond performance implications, the aesthetic aspect of your build can also suffer. Protective films on cases or transparent side panels, if not peeled off, detract from the visual appeal of your setup, leaving it looking unfinished or clouded.

Therefore, as you assemble your PC, meticulously inspect each component for any protective materials. Make it a habit to remove these before installation to ensure your system not only operates at its best but also looks its best. This simple step is crucial for achieving optimal thermal performance and maintaining the aesthetics of your build.

6 Ignoring fan placement, orientation, and dust management

Correct fan positioning and orientation directly impact system temperatures and overall performance. Incorrect fan installation, driven by the desire for aesthetics over functionality, can lead to a setup that functions as a dust magnet due to negative pressure. This common mistake turns your PC into an effective air filter for the room, capturing dust and fur, especially when placed near the floor.

To avoid such scenarios, consider the following guidelines:

  • Fan orientation: Ensure that fans are correctly oriented to support a balanced airflow within the case. Intake fans should draw cool air in, while exhaust fans expel hot air out. Misalignment can cause the recirculation of hot air and increased temperatures.
  • Avoid negative pressure: Striving for a look that showcases the fans' attractive side can inadvertently create negative pressure, drawing in unfiltered air and dust. Aim for a positive or neutral air pressure setup by balancing intake and exhaust fan numbers and placements.
  • Elevate your PC: Keeping your PC off the floor, particularly away from carpeted areas, significantly reduces dust and fur accumulation. An elevated position promotes better airflow and minimizes debris intake.
  • Regular cleaning: Schedule routine cleanings and downtimes to remove accumulated dust from filters, fans, and other components. This maintenance is essential for sustaining airflow efficiency and cooling performance, ensuring your system remains clean, cool, and visually appealing. You can do this using an airblower and a brush but make sure that while applying pressurized air none of the fans spin, failing to do so might cause the fan header to burn out.

By prioritizing effective fan placement and dust management, you can maintain optimal temperatures and longevity for your PC components, all while keeping the system's appearance sharp and dust-free.

5 Forgetting to install the I/O shield

A crucial, yet sometimes overlooked step in PC assembly is the installation of the motherboard I/O shield. This metal plate, often included with your motherboard, plays a vital role in protecting the motherboard against electromagnetic interference (EMI) and static discharge. It also prevents dust and debris from entering the system, which could lead to damage or decreased performance over time. Additionally, an I/O shield gives the back of your computer a neat, finished appearance by covering the unsightly gaps around the I/O ports​​​​​​.

While many modern motherboards come with a pre-installed I/O shield, not all do. If you're working with a motherboard that requires manual I/O shield installation, it's imperative to insert the shield into your case before mounting the motherboard. Failing to do so means you might have to disassemble major parts of your build just to fit the shield in place, wasting time and effort​.

Installation is straightforward, simply align the shield with the case's cut-out from the inside, ensuring the correct orientation by matching the port openings on the shield with those on the motherboard. Apply even pressure until the shield clicks into place. It's essential to ensure that the metal tabs or prongs on the shield lightly touch the metal on the motherboard's I/O panel for proper grounding and to reduce electrical interference​​​​​.

4 Forgetting to properly install standoffs and overtightening screws

Standoffs, typically made of brass, ensure there is no direct contact between the motherboard's circuitry and the metal case, preventing potential short circuits and providing a grounding point. Most cases come with pre-installed standoffs, but if your case doesn't align with your motherboard's holes, you may need to adjust or add standoffs manually​​.

When it comes to screwing the motherboard and components into place, the consensus is to tighten the screws until they are snug but not overly tight. Over-tightening can strip the brass standoffs or damage the motherboard by crushing the PCB. The screws should be turned just enough to hold the board firmly in place without requiring excessive force. This approach prevents potential damage to the motherboard and ensures a secure installation without risking the components.

3 Mixing up 8pin EPS and PCIe power connectors

Mixing up the 8-pin EPS and PCIe power connectors is a common mistake due to their similar appearances. However, they serve different purposes and are not interchangeable. The EPS connector is specifically designed for CPU power on the motherboard, providing the necessary power for the processor to function properly. On the other hand, the PCIe power connector is intended for graphics cards and some PCIe devices, delivering additional power these components need to operate​​​​​.

Despite looking nearly identical, these connectors have key differences in their pin configurations to prevent incorrect connections. The EPS-12V connector typically has two square-shaped pins in one row, while the PCIe 8-pin connector has a different arrangement with one square-shaped pin for pins 5-8, designed to prevent them from fitting into the wrong slot. Attempting to force a connector into the incorrect slot can damage the components​​​.

Forgetting to connect the EPS power can also lead to significant issues, including the computer not booting at all. This connector is often located in a less visible area of the motherboard, possibly obscured by fans or radiators, making it easy to overlook. Realizing this mistake after the system is assembled can result in wasted time and effort, as you may need to disassemble parts of your build to access and connect the EPS power properly​.

Always double-check the labels on your cables and connectors to ensure the correct usage, and remember, when in doubt, refer to your power supply and motherboard manuals for guidance. This careful attention to detail can save you from potential hardware damage and the frustration of troubleshooting preventable power issues.

2 Installing RAM in the wrong slots

Installing RAM in the correct slots on your motherboard is crucial for optimizing the memory bandwidth and taking full advantage of dual or quad-channel memory configurations. The placement of RAM modules directly influences whether your system operates in single, dual, or even quad-channel mode, which in turn affects the overall performance of your PC.

For motherboards with four RAM slots, a common configuration for dual-channel memory is to insert RAM modules into slots 2 and 4. This setup allows the CPU to access the RAM modules faster, thereby enhancing system performance. If you're using a single RAM stick, it's typically advised to install it in the slot that's farthest from the CPU to avoid potential interference with CPU coolers or other components​​​.

For dual-channel RAM, it's important to place matching RAM modules in the correct slots, often indicated by color coding or labeling (e.g., DIMM1, DIMM2, etc.) on the motherboard.

This ensures that the memory modules operate in dual-channel mode, doubling the data transfer rate compared to a single-channel setup. The exact slots can vary depending on the motherboard manufacturer and model, so consulting the motherboard's manual is essential for the correct installation​.

If your motherboard supports more than four RAM slots, indicating potential triple-channel or quad-channel memory support, the slot configuration will differ. In such cases, referring to the motherboard's manual is again crucial to determining the correct installation process for maximizing memory performance.

1 Not budgeting properly for software and peripherals

When planning a PC build, it's crucial not to allocate your entire budget to hardware components alone, neglecting the cost of the operating system (OS), software, and peripherals necessary for a complete setup. A common oversight is underestimating or forgetting to budget for these essential elements, leading to a scenario where you might have a powerful build but lack the software or peripherals to fully utilize it.

Moreover, peripherals such as monitors, keyboards, mice, and possibly speakers or headsets can vary widely in price. Monitors, especially, can be a significant portion of your budget, with prices ranging from $100 to several thousand dollars, depending on the specifications. Keyboards, mice, and headsets also have a broad price range, from around $20-$30 to $200-$300, based on performance and features​.

Therefore, when planning your PC build, it's wise to list all components, software, and peripherals you'll need and allocate your budget accordingly. This holistic approach ensures you don't end up with a high-end PC that you can't fully enjoy due to missing software or peripherals.

Bonus: Not choosing the correct tools and workspace

Choosing the correct tools and workspace is extremely important for a smooth PC building process, ensuring safety and efficiency. To start, a clean, spacious, and well-lit area serves as an ideal workspace, minimizing the risk of losing parts or damaging components. An anti-static mat and wrist strap are essential to prevent electrostatic discharge, protecting sensitive hardware.

For tools, a set of precision screwdrivers, preferably magnetized to handle small screws easily, is necessary. Tweezers or needle-nose pliers can help place or retrieve small items, and scissors or a box cutter are useful for opening packages. Keep cable ties or velcro straps handy for cable management, promoting a tidy build.

Remember, a well-organized workspace and the right set of tools not only facilitate a smoother building experience but also contribute to the overall success and longevity of your custom PC. Ensure you have everything prepared before starting your build to avoid unnecessary interruptions or hardware issues.

Building a PC requires attention to detail, from selecting a power supply that ensures stability and efficiency to ensuring compatibility among all components. Pre-assembly testing, or "breadboarding," is vital for identifying any potential issues before final assembly. Additionally, not removing protective covers or mismanaging fan placement can adversely affect both aesthetics and performance. Equally important is budgeting for not just the hardware but also essential software and peripherals to fully realize the potential of your build. This comprehensive approach ensures a smooth building process, resulting in a system that meets your performance needs and aesthetic preferences, providing a rewarding experience that maximizes both the longevity and efficacy of your custom PC.

item.title: Google Chrome will soon protect your home network from cyberattacks
item.itunes:title: Google Chrome will soon protect your home network from cyberattacks
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Google Chrome will soon have a new feature to block malicious hacking attempts on your network.
  • The update will check the source of website connections to prevent unauthorized access to devices.
  • When it's released, the new feature will only warn the user about the connection, and will likely evolve into blocking them automatically.

With web browsers acting as the portal between yourself and the internet, it must come with defense mechanisms to prevent hackers from gaining a foothold on your computer or network. As such, Google is stepping up its game by making Chrome even more secure. In an upcoming update, Google Chrome will be able to detect and block hacking attempts where a malicious agent tries to gain a foothold on the target's network and infect other devices.

Related
Acer Chromebox CXI5: Price, availability, and everything you need to know
The Acer Chromebox CXI5 is the first new Chromebox in a while, but it comes with new upgrades and even an optional monitor to make it more exciting.

Google Chrome's upcoming cybersecurity feature

As stated on the Chrome Platform Status website, the update will add a new feature that will perform a quick check before allowing a website access to your network devices. If a website tries to connect to a device on your home network, Chrome will ensure that the request is from a secure source and that the target device allows for connections of this nature. If implemented properly, this should prevent hackers from reaching out to the devices on your home network through a website.

However, this feature may end up blocking legitimate, desired connections instead of just the malicious ones. As such, Google will give the technology a test run first, where it can detect these connections and show a warning instead of outright blocking them:

The above checks are made to protect the user's private network. Since this feature is the "warning-only" mode, we do not fail the requests if any of the checks fails. Instead, a warning will be shown in the DevTools, to help developers prepare for the coming enforcement.

Once all the bugs have been ironed out and developers have tweaked their apps to work with the new technology, Google will likely allow the technology to begin blocking these connections. Until then, it's a good idea to browse smart and use a good antivirus to keep yourself safe.
